hints: Only the relative order of the 4 DaBaby and 6 Rae songs matters.
All Rae before the 2nd DaBaby ⟺ at least 3 DaBaby songs come after the last Rae.
Favorable D-position sets: 7 of C(10,4)=210 ⇒ 1/30.
solution: Among the $4$ DaBaby ($D$) and $6$ Rae ($R$) songs, the event is that the $2$nd $D$ falls after the last $R$, i.e. at least $3$ $D$'s trail all $R$'s. Counting $D$-position sets in $\binom{10}{4}=210$: all four trailing ($1$) plus exactly three trailing with one $D$ among the first six ($6$) gives $7$, so $P=\tfrac{7}{210}=\tfrac1{30}.$

hints: Correlation is invariant to positive scaling and shifts.
8X scales positively; +7 is a shift.
Correlation stays 0.56.
solution: Correlation is unchanged by positive scaling and additive shifts, so $\mathrm{corr}(8X,Y+7)=\mathrm{corr}(X,Y)=0.56.$

hints: After the first flip, each new flip stops the game (matches previous) w.p. 1/2.
P(stop at toss k) = (1/2)^{k−1} for k≥2.
solution: The game stops at toss $k$ if the first $k-1$ flips alternate and the $k$-th matches the previous, with probability $(\tfrac12)^{k-1}$ for $k\ge2$. Summing over even $k$: $\tfrac12+\tfrac18+\cdots=\dfrac{1/2}{1-1/4}=\dfrac23.$

hints: The top two meet in the final iff they start in opposite halves of the 128-player bracket.
Given player 1's slot, 64 of the other 127 slots are in the opposite half.
solution: The two strongest players reach the final iff they are in different halves of the draw. Fixing the top seed, $64$ of the remaining $127$ positions lie in the opposite half, so $P=\tfrac{64}{127}.$

hints: Trump wins ⟺ he wins all three states, so 'PA Republican' pays 1 whenever 'Trump yes' does — and sometimes more.
Short Trump-yes (+0.17) and buy PA-Republican (−0.16); payoffs never net negative.
solution: Since Trump wins only if he wins all three states, 'PA Republican' pays $\$1$ in every state where 'Trump yes' pays $\$1$, and possibly more. Shorting Trump-yes (credit $0.17$) and buying PA-Republican (cost $0.16$) is never net-negative at expiry, for a riskless credit of $0.17-0.16=\$0.01.$

hints: Need x₁,x₂ in different sub-brackets: prob 2^{n−1}/(2ⁿ−1).
Each wins its own n−1 rounds (each as favorite, prob p): p^{n−1} each; then x₂ beats x₁ w.p. 1−p.
solution: They meet in the final with probability $\dfrac{2^{n-1}}{2^n-1}$. Each then wins its own $n-1$ rounds as the favorite ($p^{n-1}$ each), and $x_2$ beats $x_1$ with probability $1-p$. So the probability is $\dfrac{2^{n-1}}{2^n-1}p^{2(n-1)}(1-p)$; at $n=4,p=\tfrac34$: $\dfrac{8}{15}\big(\tfrac{27}{64}\big)^2\tfrac14=\dfrac{243}{10240}.$

hints: The paid value is uniform on {2,3,4,5,6} (conditioned on not being 1).
Average those five values.
solution: The last roll is uniform on $\{2,3,4,5,6\}$, so the fair value is $\dfrac{2+3+4+5+6}{5}=4.$

hints: Let e_i be expected rounds from i distinct faces; each round rolls 2 dice gaining 0, 1, or 2 new faces.
e_i = 1 + P₀e_i + P₁e_{i+1} + P₂e_{i+2}, with the gain probabilities from i.
Solving down from e₆=0 gives e₀ = 70219/9240.
solution: Let $e_i$ be the expected rounds with $i$ faces collected. Rolling two dice from state $i$ gains $0,1,2$ new faces with probabilities $\tfrac{i^2}{36},\,\tfrac{(6-i)(1+2i)}{36},\,\tfrac{(6-i)(5-i)}{36}$, giving $e_i=1+P_0e_i+P_1e_{i+1}+P_2e_{i+2}$. Solving from $e_6=0$ yields $e_0=\dfrac{70219}{9240}.$

hints: After n rolls the payout is αⁿ with probability (5/6)ⁿ, else 0.
Expected payout (5/6)ⁿαⁿ is constant (=1) only if α(5/6)=1.
solution: After $n$ rolls the expected payout is $\alpha^n(\tfrac56)^n$. For this to stay constant at the starting value $1$ for all $n$, we need $\tfrac56\alpha=1$, i.e. $\alpha=\tfrac65.$

hints: Var(Y)=25c²+100(1−c)² by independence.
Differentiate: 50c−200(1−c)=0.
solution: By independence $\mathrm{Var}(Y)=25c^2+100(1-c)^2$. Setting the derivative $50c-200(1-c)=0$ gives $250c=200$, so $c=\tfrac45.$

hints: Count length-n sequences whose first HH is at the end: these number F_{n−1} (Fibonacci).
solution: The number of length-$n$ sequences in which the first 'HH' occurs exactly at the end is the Fibonacci number $F_{n-1}$, so $P=\dfrac{F_{n-1}}{2^n}$. For $n=10$, $F_9=34$, giving $\dfrac{34}{1024}=\dfrac{17}{512}.$

hints: The fraction of red cards remaining is a martingale.
Any stopping rule has expected success equal to the starting red fraction.
= 26/52 = 1/2; no strategy beats it.
solution: The proportion of red cards remaining in the deck is a martingale, so by optional stopping every strategy yields expected win probability equal to the initial red fraction, $\tfrac{26}{52}=\tfrac12$. No rule can do better, so the answer is $\tfrac12.$
